creator: raimeetravel
caption:
POV: attending an ancient world festival at Stonehenge for the summer solstice! 

I finally experienced the summer solstice at Stonehenge last year and it was one of the most unique events I’ve ever attended!

If you’re thinking about going, here’s what you should know:

☀️ The summer solstice marks the longest day of the year
🪨 Stonehenge was built over 4,000 years ago and is aligned with the solstice sunrise (& no one really knows why it was built that way or how!!)
🚶‍♀️ This is one of the only times each year visitors can walk right up to the stones
🎟️ The event is completely free to attend
🌅 Sunrise is around 5am, but many people arrive the night before
🚆 Coming from London? Take the train to Salisbury, then use the special shuttle buses to Stonehenge
🧥 Bring warm layers. Even in June it gets surprisingly cold overnight
🎉 Expect a mix of history lovers, spiritual groups, photographers, travelers, and curious locals
👥 Tens of thousands of people attend, so transportation and parking should be planned well in advance

Would you stay up all night for this?!!
.
.
.
Solstice, June 21, summer travel, England travel, Stonehenge

transcription.text: Every year on the morning of June 21st, this ancient tradition takes place at Stonehenge. It's one of the only times you can walk up to the stones instead of being behind a barrier. Thousands of people from all over the world come to gather to celebrate the solstice. It's an all-night celebration, and it's one of the coolest travel experiences I've ever had. So if you want to go, here's what you should know. If you're coming from London, this is not just a normal day trip. Most people arrive late at night or very early in the morning because sunrise is around 5am. You can drive, book a coach tour, or take a train to Salisbury. There are special shuttle buses running for the event. A few other things to know. Bring layers because it gets cold overnight. Expect huge crowds and don't count on getting much sleep. There are a lot of people, so if you don't want to get stuck waiting in line for the buses to take you back to the train station, I would drive. But just make sure to reserve your parking spot in advance. Watching the sunrise over a monument that's been standing for over 4,000 years is one of the most surreal experiences you can have in England. Do you think you would stay up all night for this?
